entry.title: Washington Post: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Russia-Ukraine war live updates: Ukraine claws back part of Severodonetsk as Russia continues assault
  • For Hong Kong students, commemorating Tiananmen may now require ‘hide and seek’
  • Ukrainian troops locked in brutal combat for key city as Putin believes time on his side
  • ‘They’re in hell’: Hail of Russian artillery tests Ukrainian morale
  • Saudi dissidents call Biden’s planned visit to kingdom a betrayal
  • Harry and Meghan make first public appearance at queen’s Platinum Jubilee
  • Zelensky shares defiant video after 100 days of war in Ukraine
  • Japan records its largest natural population decline as births fall
  • All the queen’s horses: From her first pony to Macron’s jubilee gift
  • Scenes of ruin and resilience from 100 days of war in Ukraine
  • North Korea heads U.N.-linked disarmament group amid consternation
  • Making sense of North Korea’s covid mystery — and its menace
  • Two future kings set to honor Elizabeth at jubilee concert
  • Supreme Leader acknowledges Iran took oil from Greek tankers
  • High-speed train derailment in China kills 1, injures 8
  • Pageant participants in queen's jubilee celebrate diverse UK
  • Abu Dhabi hosts a pandemic-delayed Bollywood awards ceremony
  • 2 UN peacekeepers killed in 6th incident in Mali in 2 weeks
  • Colombia tells Mexican leader to butt out of president race
  • Fighting rages in two key eastern Ukrainian cities
  • Grain supply tops Putin's talks with African Union leader
  • Dozens injured in train crash in northwestern Slovakia
  • Empty clinics, no calls: The fallout of Oklahoma’s abortion ban
  • DeSantis makes it harder for transgender people to get Medicaid support
  • Man breaks into Dallas art museum, damages ancient artifacts, police say
  • There have been over 200 mass shootings so far in 2022
  • Young men, guns and the prefrontal cortex
  • Escaped inmate dies in shootout hours after allegedly killing five
  • Ex-U.S. diplomat pleads guilty in Qatar lobbying plot, names general
  • Former Trump adviser Peter Navarro charged with contempt of Congress
  • D.C. Proud Boy and bartender pleads guilty to felony in Capitol riot
  • Post Politics Now: Biden says strong jobs report shows U.S. well-positioned to tackle inflation
  • Biden reportedly to visit Saudi Arabia, which he once vowed to make a ‘pariah’
  • Economy shows resilience despite mounting recession fears
  • Unemployment rate stays steady at pandemic low of 3.6 percent
  • William E. Stoney Jr., NASA engineer during space race, dies at 96
  • Dave Clark, the executive who helped build Amazon’s delivery network, is stepping down
  • Here’s How US States Differ on Carrying Guns in Public
  • Understanding Monkeypox and How Outbreaks Spread
  • The Post endorses Kelly Schulz in the GOP primary for Maryland governor
  • Yes, we can do something about gun violence
  • The Medicare and Social Security disaster that Washington is doing nothing to fix
  • Georgetown Law did the right thing on Ilya Shapiro
  • The next step in a long march: Expanding mobile voting in D.C.
  • Instead of Dollar Homes, a reparation zone in Baltimore
  • Is Jamestown worth saving?
  • Metro has a chance to break out of its tailspin of turmoil
  • Ask Eugene Robinson about the latest news around the U.S. and world
  • Chat with Perry Bacon Jr. about politics and beyond
  • Chat with Jennifer Rubin about her columns, politics, policy and more
  • Send a letter to the editor
  • Contact the reader representative
  • Submit an op-ed
Breaking news and analysis on politics, business, world national news, entertainment more. In-depth DC, Virginia, Maryland news coverage including traffic, weather, crime, education, restaurant reviews and more.

entry.title: NY Times: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • A Farmer Holds On, a Fraying Lifeline for a Besieged Corner of Ukraine
  • 100 Days of War in Ukraine: Death, Destruction and Loss
  • For Families in Uvalde, Laws Limit Paths to Holding Police Accountable

  • London Police Evacuate Trafalgar Square, but Say ‘Incident Has Now Been Concluded’

  • To Cut Gas Prices, Congress Should Allow the U.S. to Sue OPEC

  • One Island, Two Men and Lots of Big Questions
  • Live Updates: Length of Front Line Puts Pressure on Ukraine

  • Thousands Swept Up as Kremlin Clamps Down on War Criticism
  • U.K.’s ‘Brightest and Best’ Visa Leaves Out Africa, India and Latin America
  • In the Philippines, a Doctor Unearths a Drug War’s True Toll

  • Israel Builds a Laser Weapon to Zap Threats Out of the Sky

  • Zelensky Tells U.S. Mayors to End Their Sister-City Ties with Russia
  • ‘We Have Not Forgotten Syria’: U.S. Envoy Vows to Protect Refugees

  • Covid Vaccine Misinformation Still Fuels Fears Surrounding Pregnancy, a New Study Finds.
  • 21 Americans Infected With Monkeypox, C.D.C. Reports
  • Most Canadians Want to See the British Monarchy Come to an End
  • Carbon Dioxide Levels Are Highest in Human History
  • Could Australia and New Zealand ‘Divorce’ From the British Monarchy?
  • Alameda County in California Reinstates Indoor Mask Mandate
  • Killing in Wisconsin Was Motivated by Judicial Matter, Attorney General Says
  • Democrats Face Pressure on Crime From a New Front: Their Base
  • Mariachis Ride Into Uvalde With Songs of Heartache and Hope
  • 3 G.O.P. Candidates for Michigan Governor Will Stay Off Ballot, a Top Court Rules
  • North Carolina TV stations pull an attack ad against Cheri Beasley, a Democrat running for Senate.
  • Is Jan. 6 a Winning Political Issue in California? We’re About to Find Out.
  • Gina Haspel Observed Waterboarding at C.I.A. Black Site, Psychologist Testifies
  • Pennsylvania Court Orders Undated Ballots to Be Counted, Siding With McCormick For Now
  • Tesla to Cut 10% of Salaried Staff, Musk Tells Employees
  • What Depp Trial Means for Media Companies’ Airing #MeToo Accusations
  • When Your Colleagues’ Mental Health Is on the Agenda
  • Hiring Remains Strong Even as Fed Tries to Cool Economy
  • Fossil-Fuel Shares Lead the Stock Market. How Awkward.
  • Apple Sees Virtually-Reality Headset as Its Next Big Thing

entry.title: Guardian: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • England v New Zealand: first Test, day three – live!
  • Russia-Ukraine war: Ukraine says Russian forces suffering ‘huge losses’ as it fights back in Sievierodonetsk – live
  • Japanese man, 83, becomes oldest person to sail solo non-stop across Pacific

  • Escape from Auschwitz: the most extraordinary Holocaust story you’ve never heard

  • Race at the Top: white and Asian Americans and the push for equity in education

  • How to turn squishy tomatoes into delicious jam - recipe | Waste not

  • UK officials in line for immunity in assisting crimes overseas, say critics
  • Blind date: ‘It was going well until he choked on his dessert and started crying’
  • Tim Dowling: I wonder what my lovely aunt would’ve made of her funeral

  • Nations League: Denmark stun France, Netherlands take apart Belgium
  • England shocked by Hungary hosting 36,000 for Nations League despite ban
  • ‘Like a bridge that connects us’: Pride parade comes to Bangkok amid new hope for LGBT rights
  • Can southern sushi glow up? The cheese roll debate dividing New Zealand’s deep south
  • Ben Roberts-Smith: a war hero’s reputation at stake in Australia’s defamation ‘trial of the century’
  • Beyond our ‘ape-brained meat sacks’: can transhumanism save our species?
  • Secret Service were warned of security risk to Pence day before Capitol attack
  • What happened in the Russia-Ukraine war this week? Catch up with the must-read news and analysis
  • Fifth case of monkeypox discovered in New York City
  • EU imposes sanctions on Russian oil boss linked to seized superyachts
  • Uefa apologises to Liverpool and Madrid fans over Champions League chaos
  • Hillary Clinton faced constant sexism in 2016 campaign, says ex-aide
  • Interceptor review – absurd yet entertaining Netflix action thriller
  • French police find machine gun arsenal after arresting neo-Nazi suspects in Alsace
  • Myanmar junta says it will execute two prominent pro-democracy leaders
  • California allows driverless taxi service to operate in San Francisco
  • Cocktail of the week: Drake & Morgan’s royal summertime – recipe | The good mixer

entry.title: South China Morning Post: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: South China Morning Post: 4 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-04 10:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Hong Kong couple arrested over murder of 14-month-old baby girl, after child found with crescent-shaped marks on neck
  • Hong Kong must open up borders in ‘prudent’ manner, commerce chief says, as city records 446 Covid cases
  • Hong Kong government expresses ‘strong opposition’ to US criticism of religious freedom in city
  • Incoming Hong Kong leader John Lee vows to uphold revamped electoral system to pave way for economic development and resolve housing, livelihood issues
  • Time to raise Hong Kong’s minimum wage? Businesses cite pandemic-hit economy as reason for non-review, amid calls to raise hourly mark from HK$37.5 to HK$50
  • Beijing warns Western envoys in Hong Kong not to openly commemorate Tiananmen Square crackdown on anniversary of event
  • Hong Kong’s butcher heartthrob has the city swooning – like boy band Mirror, he’s making people feel they have something to be proud of
  • Remembering the Tiananmen Square crackdown: with no June 4 vigil in Hong Kong, will memories fade or can overseas events carry the torch?
  • Don’t let Hong Kong’s need for speed on housing crisis wreck Fanling golf course
  • New history textbooks for Hong Kong students ‘compress details of Tiananmen Square crackdown, with no mention of commemorative vigil’
  • Sections of Hong Kong’s Victoria Park to close on June 4 after man arrested over online calls to attack police
  • Telegram shuts down 2 channels created under same name as account blocked for alleged doxxing after Hong Kong privacy watchdog report
  • China’s top e-commerce influencer, ‘Lipstick King’ Austin Li Jiaqi, abruptly ends Taobao show in major live-streaming snafu
  • No autopilot: China says Australia ties reset calls for ‘concrete actions’ from new government
  • One dead in high-speed train crash in southwest China
  • China’s Shenzhou 14 astronaut trio ready for lift-off on Sunday, space agency confirms
  • China’s Covid-19 era students prepare for gaokao college exams in record numbers
  • China’s coronavirus-induced fall in marriages sees firms target singles as consumption landscape evolves
  • Habitual tea drinking may reduce the effects of chronic insomnia, study finds
  • Eye of the beholder: Will security and human-rights concerns get Chinese camera makers Hikvision and Dahua banned in the UK?
  • Russia’s urban warfare struggles in Ukraine ‘a key lesson for China’ in possible Taiwan conflict
  • No 2 US diplomat’s trip to show US ‘continued commitment to Indo-Pacific’
  • China’s shipping industry readies for surge in demand as Shanghai restarts its export machine
  • No sign of China’s new supercomputers among world’s Top500
  • Dragon boat races return to southern China as Covid cases retreat
  • China-Hong Kong ‘round-tripping investment’ remains vital as economy slows, foreign business confidence tumbles
  • Taiwan reports 142 more deaths as it considers easing quarantine on entry
  • China delays launch of Type 003 aircraft carrier for unknown reason – are technical problems at fault?
  • Indonesian haj pilgrims arrive in Saudi, first since before pandemic
  • Japan PM plans to attend Nato summit in first for country’s leader
  • Fears for Indonesia’s Sumatran elephants after pregnant animal dies of suspected poisoning
  • Japanese man, 83, becomes oldest person to sail solo across Pacific Ocean
  • What Singapore is doing to boost food security, from urban farms to homegrown vegetables
  • Can the world spend its way out of the current global food crisis?
  • China must switch from mass testing to vaccinations to move on from its wasteful zero-Covid policy
  • From Malaysia to India, how are export bans on chicken, wheat, sugar affecting Asia?
  • Free as a bird: Bali breeders roped in to help endangered mynahs back into Indonesian wild
  • As major powers fight, the rest of the world must learn to help themselves
  • Australia fears foot-and-mouth risk as Indonesia outbreak infects 20,000 animals
  • ‘Truly tragic accident’ of Singapore couple and son killed by improperly installed water heater
  • Use price boom to support greener palm oil production, urges global watchdog’s new chief
  • Myanmar junta to execute 4, including democracy activist and ex-member of Suu Kyi’s party
  • As Malaysia’s forests disappear, one dedicated man steps up to save its orchids
  • Bill Cosby forcibly kissed me when I was 14, says 61-year-old woman in US court
  • How electronic warfare is shaping the Russia-Ukraine war
  • Why there’s more US mass shootings in warmer weather
  • US troops linked to attacks on cats in Hawaii
  • Trump-backed Dr Oz wins Republican primary for Senate race in Pennsylvania
  • Taser developer Axon proposes armed drones to stop US school shootings
  • Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelensky vows victory on 100th day of Russian invasion
  • Russia’s Vladimir Putin says ‘no problem’ to export grain from Ukraine, amid food crisis fears
  • Former Trump aide Peter Navarro indicted for defying Capitol riot subpoena
  • At least 4 killed as train derails in southern German Alps
  • Diversity pressure, listing rules will lead to greater representation of women on boards of Hong Kong-listed firms, says executive search veteran
  • Inflation will drag down growth, but US should avoid recession this year
  • Hong Kong homebuyers mark Dragon Boat Festival by buying all flats at Sun Hung Kai’s Silicon Hill project in New Territories
  • How China’s indie filmmakers struggle to bring the stories – and languages – of rural China to the big screen
  • Why you should spend time outdoors and in nature – it has benefits for your mind and body, happiness and creativity
  • How to make coronation chicken, served in 1953 to mark Queen Elizabeth’s ascent to the throne, and the story behind the dish
  • How turning The New Yorker into The Hong Konger sparked the digital art career of Sophia Hotung, after years of illness had snuffed out her corporate ambitions
  • Trekking Nepal: 6 of the best ‘teahouse’ treks in Nepal, including Everest Base Camp
  • K-drama midseason recap: Woori the Virgin – melodramatic romcom starring Lim Soo-hyang slows down after impressive start
  • Overrun by hotels, resorts, bars, Bali’s new travel hotspots fall one by one to uncontrolled development on west coast
  • The future of Covid-19 vaccines? Why nasal sprays, not needles, could be better in the long run
  • How will Johnny Depp and Amber Heard rebuild their careers and restore their public images?
  • Buddhist nuns Jeong Kwan and Wookwan named Masters of Korean temple food for their roles in popularising the cuisine
  • How male celebs are levelling up their red carpet jewellery: Timothée Chalamet dazzled in Cartier at this year’s Oscars, while Pharrell Williams kicked off the trend in Chanel pearls and diamonds
  • Inside Lionel Messi’s luxury car collection, from his Mercedes SUV and Cadillac Escalade fit for a US president … and even a rare US$4 million Pagani Zonda that’s illegal to drive?
  • Who is the richest Star Wars cast member in 2022? Net worths, ranked – from Ewan McGregor and Hayden Christensen reprising roles in Disney+’s Obi-Wan Kenobi, to Harrison Ford and Natalie Portman
  • Romeo Beckham and Mia Regan: the next Posh and Becks? Inside the Gen Z model and athlete’s romance – and their fashion-football similarities with Victoria and David
SCMP.com, Hong Kong's premier online English daily provides exclusive up-to-date news, audio video news, podcasts, RSS Feeds, Blogs, breaking news, top stories, award winning news and analysis on Hong Kong and China.

entry.title: Asahi Shimbun: 4 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

The Asahi Shimbun is widely regarded for its journalism as the most respected daily newspaper in Japan. The English version offers selected articles from the vernacular Asahi Shimbun, as well as extensive coverage of cool Japan,focusing on manga, travel and other timely news.

  • What The Matrix Got Wrong About Cities of the Future
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

  • Extreme storms will grow bigger as the world warms
  • A harmless bird virus shows ominous changes
  • How dazzling bacteria get their rainbow
  • The salamander that can parachute from atop the world’s tallest trees
  • Gene-edited tomatoes, South Asian heatwave and Australian election
  • Monkeypox goes global: why scientists are on alert
  • ‘Mind blowing’ ancient settlements uncovered in the Amazon
  • Why unprecedented bird flu outbreaks sweeping the world are concerning scientists
  • COVID delays are frustrating the world’s plans to save biodiversity
  • Frustration builds over lengthy delay in revamping Mexico’s science law
  • Guardians of the brain: how a special immune system protects our grey matter
  • Chronic pain — why science has scant succour for one in five people
  • Animal power, and how to perpetuate wonder: Books in brief
  • A century of science boycotts
  • Fifty years after UN environment summit, researchers renew call for action
  • Has the ‘great resignation’ hit academia?
  • How engineers and drug developers are working to change childhood cancer’s deadly calculus
  • A down-to-earth approach to climate change
  • Loops simplify a set-up to boost quantum computational advantage
  • Nerve regrowth can be painful
  • The 6G frequency switch that spares scientific services
  • Clues to the identity of the fossil fish Palaeospondylus
  • From the archive: international efforts to curb pollution, and the perils of broadcasting
  • Multilayer 2D insulator shows promise for post-silicon electronics
  • An immune molecule segregates memories in time
  • The road to fully programmable protein catalysis
  • Strongly correlated electron–photon systems
  • Resolving the H i in damped Lyman α systems that power star formation
  • Probing CP symmetry and weak phases with entangled double-strange baryons
  • Magneto-optical trapping and sub-Doppler cooling of a polyatomic molecule
  • Quantum computational advantage with a programmable photonic processor
  • Non-Hermitian chiral phononics through optomechanically induced squeezing
  • Epitaxial single-crystal hexagonal boron nitride multilayers on Ni (111)
  • A tissue-like neurotransmitter sensor for the brain and gut
  • [18F]Difluorocarbene for positron emission tomography
  • Morphology of Palaeospondylus shows affinity to tetrapod ancestors
  • The mosaic oat genome gives insights into a uniquely healthy cereal crop
  • Single-cell eQTL models reveal dynamic T cell state dependence of disease loci
  • People construct simplified mental representations to plan
  • Neuropathic pain caused by miswiring and abnormal end organ targeting
  • CCR5 closes the temporal window for memory linking
  • Gene regulation by gonadal hormone receptors underlies brain sex differences
  • Distinct gene clusters drive formation of ferrosome organelles in bacteria
  • Developmental dynamics of two bipotent thymic epithelial progenitor types
  • Fundamental immune–oncogenicity trade-offs define driver mutation fitness
  • Mitochondrial uncouplers induce proton leak by activating AAC and UCP1
  • Gibbin mesodermal regulation patterns epithelial development
  • MCM complexes are barriers that restrict cohesin-mediated loop extrusion
  • Fast and efficient DNA replication with purified human proteins
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nauture also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: New Yorker: June 6, 2022

Articles in this issue:

entry.title: Asian Review of Books: 2 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: Asian Review of Books: 2 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-02 23:40PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • “Thank You, Mr Nixon” by Gish Jen
  • Podcast with Erin Murphy, author of “Burmese Haze: US Policy and Myanmar’s Opening—and Closing”
  • New Book Announcement: “Water Thicker Than Blood: A Memoir of a Post-Internment Childhood” by George Uba
  • “Picking Off New Shoots Will Not Stop the Spring”, edited by Ko Ko Thett and Brian Haman
  • Opera in brief: Musica Viva’s abridged Giulio Cesare
  • New Book Announcement: “The Substance of Fiction: Literary Objects in China, 1550–1775” by Sophie Volpp
  • “I Was the President Mistress!!” by Miguel Syjuco
  • New Book Announcement: “Ethical Encounters: Transnational Feminism, Human Rights, and War Cinema in Bangladesh” by Elora Halim Chowdhury
  • “In the Forest of the Blind: The Eurasian Journey of Faxian’s Record of Buddhist Kingdoms” by Matthew W King
  • “Solo Dance” by Li Kotomi
In addition to reviewing books about or of relevance to Asia, the Asian Review of Books also features long-format essays by leading Asian writers and thinkers, to providing an unparalleled forum for discussion of key contemporary issues by Asians for Asia and a vehicle of intellectual depth and breadth where leading thinkers can write on the books, arts and ideas of the day. Widely quoted and referenced, with an archive of more than one thousand book reviews, it is the only web resource dedicated to Asian books. And now, with the addition of the new premium content, the Asian Review of Books, is a must-read publication.

entry.title: London Review of Books: 9 Jun, 2022
entry.summary: London Review of Books: 9 Jun, 2022 published at 2022-06-01 13:41PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

Literary review publishing essay-length book reviews and topical articles on politics, literature, history, philosophy, science and the arts by leading writers and thinkers
